Not applicable / not collected. oneweek_pct is sourced from the California Voter Foundation 'Ballot Processing' charts, which cover California counties only — there is no CalVoter publication for Wisconsin. Context (not recorded as a value to avoid substituting a different denominator): Wisconsin counts essentially all ballots by election night or the early morning after Election Day. Municipalities report unofficial results the night of the election; central-count municipalities (e.g. Milwaukee) finish reporting absentee results in the early AM hours after polls close; county boards of canvass certify within ~1-2 weeks. A WI 'one-week-counted' figure would be ~100%, but no comparable CalVoter-basis number is published, so value=null.

Not applicable / not collected. CalVoter 'Ballot Processing' charts are California-only; Wisconsin is not covered. Wisconsin reports essentially complete unofficial results election night / early the next morning, so a one-week-counted share would be near 100%, but there is no CalVoter-basis published value to record. Value left null rather than substituting a different denominator.

adoptions.note: ADOPTED. Fresno County uses automated signature-verification software for the FIRST-pass comparison of VBM envelope signatures. Per an Oct 12, 2020 op-ed by Brandi Orth, Fresno County Clerk/Registrar of Voters: 'The Fresno County Elections Office uses a signature verification software program that completes the first comparison. After this automated comparison, approximately 50% of the signatures are compared and verified'; signatures the software cannot verify are then 'viewed first-hand by staff trained to compare signatures.' Software vendor not named in the source (Fresno's voting system is Dominion ImageCast, purchased 2019). adopted_year/first_election set to the Nov 3, 2020 General, the election during which the Registrar publicly documented the automated software in use; the software may predate that (possibly introduced with the 2019 Dominion system / March 2020 VCA launch) but no earlier primary date was located. The EAP's generic line ('Signatures on every VBM ballot envelope are compared to the voter's registration signature and must match before being counted') does not by itself indicate automation; the op-ed is the primary evidence that the first pass is software-automated. VERIFIER (2026-06): op-ed re-opened and confirmed verbatim — Brandi Orth, Fresno County Clerk/Registrar of Voters, Oct 12, 2020: 'uses a signature verification software program that completes the first comparison' (~50% auto-verified, remainder hand-reviewed), so ASV is genuinely software-automated (status remains adopted; not humans-by-eye). However the SOLE evidence is a registrar-authored op-ed published on a news outlet (gvwire.com / GV Wire), not an official registrar/SoS/CalVoter document; confidence downgraded primary -> secondary per the source-type rule. adopted_year=2020 retained as a first-election year (the Nov 2020 General the op-ed documents), not a board-vote/contract date; an earlier first-use election was not located.

adoptions.vendor: DFM Associates (electronic pollbook; vote-center check-in connected to the county Election Information Management System / EIMS)
adoptions.note: CA SoS 'Voting Technologies in Use by County' (as of Oct 10, 2025) lists Fresno's Electronic Pollbook as 'DFM' (DFM Associates). Fresno's Election Administration Plan (Jan 14, 2022, https://elections.cdn.sos.ca.gov/vca/eap/fresno-eap.pdf) describes vote-center technology: 'Vote Centers will be connected to the County's Election Information Management System (EIMS) to allow Vote Center staff to verify, in real time, the voting status of each voter,' and references a powered 'check-in station' at each vote center. Electronic pollbooks/check-in were first deployed when Fresno conducted its first all-mail/vote-center election, the March 3, 2020 Presidential Primary (53 vote centers). Like Madera, the e-pollbook function is DFM/EIMS-connected check-in rather than a dedicated tablet product (no KNOWiNK/Tenex tablet documented). Board of Supervisors adopted the Voter's Choice Act model on March 12, 2019; first VCA election March 2020.

No in-vote-center mail-ballot signature-verify-and-scan ('Sign, Scan & Go', AB 626) capability described in Fresno's Election Administration Plan. Returned VBM ballots dropped at the 53 vote centers / 65 drop boxes are transported to the central Elections Office for signature verification and central tabulation (Dominion ICC/ICE); there is no mention of verifying and scanning returned VBM envelopes at the point of return inside a vote center. AB 626 sign-scan-go is associated with Placer County, not Fresno. Finding by absence of evidence in the primary EAP (secondary confidence).

Fresno adopted the California Voter's Choice Act all-mailed-ballot + vote-center model. The Board of Supervisors voted March 12, 2019 to adopt the VCA 'in time for the 2020 elections' (CA SoS news release); Fresno was the ninth county to implement the VCA. First election conducted under the model was the March 3, 2020 Presidential Primary, with 53 vote centers and drop boxes replacing 268 one-day precinct polling places (Fresno EAP, https://elections.cdn.sos.ca.gov/vca/eap/fresno-eap.pdf). Board-vote/adoption date = March 12, 2019; first election conducted with the model = March 2020.

LA County uses MACHINE signature comparison: per LAist (2026) and CBS LA, mail-ballot signatures are 'scanned by a device that compares the signature to all signatures in a voter's registration file'; only ballots the machine can't read / no-match are pulled for human clerk review. This is automated signature verification (ASV) = adopted. ASV-year CAVEAT: adopted_year=2020 is an ESTIMATE, not from a primary source — it aligns with LA's transition to all-vote-by-mail (SB 29 / Nov 2020) when automated signature comparison was scaled at the Ballot Processing Center; exact first-election year not documented publicly. The machine/software vendor is not named in public sources (Parascript SignatureXpert is the common CA vendor, but unconfirmed for LA). Verifier should confirm year and vendor.

adoptions.vendor: KNOWiNK Poll Pad
adoptions.note: LA County RR/CC customized KnowInk ePollbooks to integrate with its county-owned VSAP system; first used countywide at Vote Centers for the March 3, 2020 Presidential Primary. ePollbook is one of the six VSAP components (BMD, BMD Manager, ISB, ePollbook, Tally). Verified Voting and LA County's own VSAP materials confirm KnowInk.

No evidence LA County does in-vote-center 'sign, verify signature on the spot, and scan into the tabulator' (AB 626 model). Mail/drop-off ballots are transported to LA's central Ballot Processing Center for signature verification and tabulation (described in CBS LA / LAist coverage of why the count is slow), i.e. NOT scanned-and-cast at the vote center. The AB 626 sign-scan-and-go pilot is associated with Placer County, not LA. Confidence secondary (absence-of-pilot + central-processing description).

LA County implemented the Voter's Choice Act (vote-center model: any-vote-center voting, multi-day early voting, all-mail ballots) beginning with the March 3, 2020 Presidential Primary, one of 15 VCA counties that cycle. Confirmed by CA SoS VCA participating-counties list and SoS news release AP20:021 ('Vote Centers Open in all Voter's Choice Act Counties').

No automated signature-verification software found. Madera's full voting-technology stack in the EAP is Dominion ICVS (ICX BMDs, Mobile Ballot Printing, image-capturing tabulation scanners) plus EIMS; the image-capturing scanners are for ballot tabulation/audit-mark, not signature matching. No ASV product appears for Madera in SoS certified-technology materials, and Madera is not among the CA counties known to run ASV software (e.g. San Bernardino, Riverside). Absent special software, CA counties perform human signature comparison under Elections Code sec. 3019; finding is by absence of evidence, hence secondary confidence.

adoptions.vendor: DFM Associates (EIMS / Vote Center App, run on networked PCs at vote centers)
adoptions.note: Vote centers use electronic rosters from the first VCA election (June 2018). EAP: 'at each Vote Center a network of personal computers will be linked to the MCROV Election Information Management System (EIMS) through a secure connection. EIMS is connected to the voter registration administered by the State of California (VoteCal)... enabling Vote Center staff to determine the registration and voter status of every voter in real time.' Note the nuance (Verified Voting, secondary, https://verifiedvoting.org/election-system/dfm-associates-eims/): EIMS is technically an election-management system, not a dedicated SoS-certified standalone e-pollbook product, but it is used in the same manner as an electronic pollbook (check in voters, confirm status, void/reissue ballots). No tablet-based e-pollbook (e.g. KNOWiNK Poll Pad) documented; check-in is via networked PCs running EIMS.

No in-vote-center mail-ballot signature-verify-and-scan ('Sign-Scan-and-Go', AB 626) capability described in Madera's Election Administration Plan. Ballots dropped at vote centers/drop boxes are transported for central processing; there is no mention of verifying and scanning returned VBM envelopes at the vote center. AB 626 sign-scan-go is associated with Placer County, not Madera. Finding by absence of evidence in the primary EAP.

adoptions.vendor: Dominion Voting Systems ImageCast (ICVS) under the Voter's Choice Act model
adoptions.note: One of the original five CA counties to adopt the Voter's Choice Act all-mail/vote-center model. Election Administration Plan: 'The Voters Choice Act authorized Madera County... to conduct any election by all mailed ballot with Vote Centers commencing in 2018. The Madera County Registrar of Voters (MCROV) adopted the VCA Model in September 2017.' Board-vote/adoption date = Sept 2017; first election conducted under the model = June 5, 2018 Statewide Primary. Confirmed by CA SoS VCA participating-counties page and CSAC 'Five Counties Weigh In on the Voters Choice Act' (https://www.counties.org/county-voice/five-counties-weigh-voters-choice-act).

No automated signature-verification software/scanner found. Napa's Challenged Ballots page describes human review: a signature challenge means 'our permanent staff cannot match the ballot signature to the voter's signature on file,' indicating staff compare envelope signatures by eye (with a second human review before a ballot is challenged/cured). No ASV vendor named anywhere in county materials. Confidence secondary (based on process description + absence of any ASV evidence).

adoptions.vendor: DFM Associates (EIMS / Vote Center App electronic roster on COTS laptops)
adoptions.note: Adopted with the Voter's Choice Act vote-center model for the June 2018 primary. Napa's Election Administration Plan states each Vote Center has a 'secure connection to the County's Election Management System (EMS), allowing Vote Center staff to verify, in real time, the voting status of each voter against the local database and against the statewide registration database known as VoteCal,' and maintains 'an electronic, continuously updated index of voters' — i.e. an electronic pollbook required by the VCA model. EAP names DFM Associates as the voter-registration/EMS vendor; Verified Voting documents DFM's EIMS / Vote Center App being used as an e-pollbook on COTS laptops by CA VCA counties (https://verifiedvoting.org/election-system/dfm-associates-eims/). Exact e-pollbook product/vendor confidence is secondary; the presence of a real-time electronic roster is primary.

No in-vote-center mail-ballot signature-verify-and-scan ('Sign-Scan-and-Go', AB 626) program found. Napa's EAP describes mail ballots returned at Vote Centers and drop boxes being processed centrally (machine opens envelopes after signature verification by Election Division staff); it does not describe scanning/tabulating a voter's returned mail ballot at the Vote Center after on-the-spot signature verification. AB 626 sign-scan-and-go was piloted elsewhere (e.g. Placer). Confidence secondary (absence of evidence in primary county materials).

Napa was one of the five original Voter's Choice Act (SB 450) pilot counties — Madera, Napa, Nevada, Sacramento, San Mateo — that first conducted all-mail / vote-center elections in 2018 (June 5, 2018 statewide primary). CA SoS VCA page confirms 'five counties adopted the VCA as part of the initial pilot' in 2018 and lists Napa among current VCA counties; Napa's EAP states the VCA authorized the county to conduct elections as 'All-Mailed Ballot/Vote Center' 'commencing in 2018.' (Napa had run all-mail elections since 2006 and Voting Assistance Centers since 2008, but the VCA vote-center model with real-time e-pollbooks began 2018.)

adoptions.vendor: unknown (high-speed envelope sorter with automated signature verification)
adoptions.note: ADOPTED finding. Nevada County's own 'Mail Ballot Processing Infographic' (primary county document) states under BALLOT SORTING that 'ballot envelopes are then sorted by precinct and passed through a signature verification machine,' and under SIGNATURE VERIFICATION that 'Election staff review signatures that were NOT automatically accepted by the signature verification machine and compare them to the voter registration database.' I.e. a machine auto-accepts a portion of signatures; only the remainder go to human review -- this is automated signature verification, not pure eyeball comparison. Corroborated by The Union, Oct 20, 2025 ('a balance of machines checking humans and humans checking machine'; 'first level of signature verification') describing the new Grass Valley ballot processing center. adopted_year is a DOCUMENTED LOWER BOUND, not a confirmed first-election year: the infographic with this language was archived on the Wayback Machine as early as 2023-06-05 (so the machine was in use by the Nov 2022 general at the latest); the 2017 Election Administration Plan does not mention a signature-verification sorter. The machine could date back to the 2018 VCA all-mail transition or a later sorter acquisition -- exact first-election year and vendor (e.g. Runbeck/BlueCrest/Pitney Bowes class) not confirmed. Flag for verification: pin the sorter purchase year and vendor via board agendas / Legistar.

adoptions.vendor: Provista ViBE
adoptions.note: CA SoS 'Voting Technologies in Use by County' (as of Oct 10, 2025) lists Nevada County's Electronic Pollbook as 'Provista ViBE'. Electronic pollbooks date to the county's first Voter's Choice Act election, the June 5, 2018 Statewide Direct Primary: the county's 2017 Election Administration Plan states each vote center will have 'an electronic mechanism for county staff to immediately access ... all relevant voter registration data' and to 'determine instantly whether a voter's ballot has already been cast' (https://www.nevadacountyca.gov/DocumentCenter/View/21900). Provista ViBE is the current certified listing; the specific e-pollbook vendor used in the original June 2018 deployment was not independently confirmed (the county used Hart eSlate/eScan tabulation in 2018 and later upgraded to Hart Verity).

No evidence of in-vote-center mail-ballot signature-verify-and-scan (AB 626 'Sign, Scan & Go', the model piloted by Placer). Nevada County processes returned mail ballots centrally: per the county Mail Ballot Processing Infographic, ballots are collected from the Postal Service, drop boxes and voting locations and 'delivered to the elections office,' then centrally sorted/signature-verified on a sorter, extracted by two-person teams, and scanned in batches. Vote-center / drop-box mail-ballot returns are transported to the central ballot processing center, not signature-verified and scanned on the spot.

Nevada County was one of the original FIVE counties to adopt the California Voter's Choice Act (all-mailed-ballot + vote-center model). The county's VCA Overview page states the Voter's Choice Act 'was passed into law in 2016 and first implemented in 2018 in Madera, Napa, Nevada, Sacramento, and San Mateo counties.' First election conducted under the model = June 5, 2018 Statewide Direct Primary (the county ran 7 vote centers, down from 48 prior polling places). Board/EAP timeline: the Election Administration Plan was drafted Oct 31, 2017 and revised Nov 27 and Dec 19, 2017 (https://www.nevadacountyca.gov/2373/Election-Administration-Plan) ahead of SoS approval; the first-election date (June 5, 2018), not the 2017 plan-adoption date, is recorded as adopted_year.

No automated signature-verification software/scanner. New York verifies absentee/mail-ballot signatures by EYE: per NCSL Table 14, 'absentee voters sign an oath on the inner envelope... The central board of canvassers compares the signature on the envelope to the signature in the registration poll record. If the signatures correspond, the ballot is accepted.' This is the bipartisan manual comparison required by NY Election Law sec. 9-209, reinforced by the 2021 statutory Notice-and-Cure process (elections.ny.gov/notice-and-cure-process), which still relies on human bipartisan review ('the bipartisan team shall keep in mind that everyone writes differently'). Humans comparing signatures = not-adopted. At the poll site, e-poll books capture a voter signature for the oath but do NOT machine-match it. Confidence secondary because the cited URL is NCSL (the underlying basis -- Election Law sec. 9-209 manual board-of-canvassers review -- is primary).

adoptions.vendor: KNOWiNK Poll Pad (NYC + many counties); Tenex and Robis also state-certified
adoptions.note: Electronic poll books (digital voter rosters / iPad or Windows check-in tablets) were authorized as part of New York's 2019 election-modernization package and deployed statewide for the FIRST-EVER early voting period (Oct 26-Nov 3, 2019) and the Nov 5, 2019 general election. 51 of 62 NY counties adopted e-poll books for 2019 early voting (voteearlyny.org). The NY State Board of Elections certified three vendor systems: KNOWiNK (St. Louis, iPad), Tenex (Florida, iPad), and Robis (Illinois, Windows); the standalone authorizing bill S508 passed the Senate 2019-02-27 (Sen. Zellnor Myrie) but the operative authority/funding rode the 2019 early-voting enactment (Election Law Art. 8 / FY2020 budget), with the state providing counties ~$10M operations + ~$14M capital for e-poll books. NYC Board of Elections approved a 4-year, $19.1M contract with KNOWiNK on 2019-07-16 and bought ~10,000 iPads, first used Oct/Nov 2019 (board-vote/contract dates noted here, not the adopted_year). adopted_year=2019 = year of first election conducted with the tech. Vendor mix is county-dependent (KNOWiNK for NYC) -> vendor attribution is secondary; e-pollbook adoption itself is primary.

No in-vote-center 'Sign-Scan-and-Go' mail-ballot signature-verify-and-scan workflow (that is a California AB 626 / Voter's Choice Act vote-center concept with no New York analogue). New York mail/absentee ballots are returned to and processed CENTRALLY by the county board of elections, where the bipartisan central board of canvassers compares the oath-envelope signature and then opens and tabulates the ballot (NCSL Table 14; NY Election Law sec. 9-209). In-person early-voting and Election-Day voters cast a ballot directly into a precinct optical scanner; they do not surrender/scan a mail-ballot envelope at the site. So no sign-scan-go process exists. Confidence secondary (the absence is inferred from NY's centralized canvass procedure, which is primary).

Not a California Voter's Choice Act-style vote-center jurisdiction: New York is NOT an all-mail state and does NOT replace Election-Day precinct poll sites with countywide vote centers. IMPORTANT NUANCE -- post-2019 e-poll books DID enable countywide EARLY-VOTING sites: in 54 of 62 NY counties a voter may use ANY early-voting location in their county (rather than an assigned site), which VOTE EARLY NY describes as 'countywide votecenters.' But this applies only to the early-voting period (first held Oct 26-Nov 3, 2019); on Election Day proper, New Yorkers are still routed to their assigned election-district poll site. Because the schema's 'vote-center' type means the VCA all-mail model (which NY has not adopted), status=not-adopted, with the countywide-early-voting-site capability flagged here for a verifier. Confidence secondary.

No automated signature-verification software/scanner. OCROV EAP: 'The images of the returned envelopes are used by staff to perform a signature comparison of the ballots and determine if the signature matches'; staff review ~10,000 signatures/hour (the 45,000/hr machine is a high-speed mail sorter, not a signature matcher). Corroborated by LAist (2024/2026): 'In Orange County, signature comparisons are done by humans -- not scanners,' and Registrar Bob Page's three-reviewer rule (https://laist.com/news/politics/voter-guides/2026-election-california-primary-how-they-verify-ballot-signatures).

adoptions.vendor: Tenex Precinct Central
adoptions.note: OCROV Election Administration Plan: 'An electronic pollbook solution chosen by OCROV is a combination of tablet (iPad) hardware devices and vendor proprietary software,' first deployed at Vote Centers for the March 3, 2020 Presidential Primary (first election under the Voter's Choice Act). Provisional ballots fell from 117,188 (Nov 2018) to 3,417 (Nov 2020). Vendor 'Tenex Precinct Central' (iPad-based ePollbook) per Verified Voting / industry reporting and CA SoS certification, not named in the EAP itself -> vendor attribution is secondary; e-pollbook adoption itself is primary.

No in-vote-center 'Sign-Scan-and-Go' / AB 626 mail-ballot signature-verify-and-scan workflow. Returned mail ballots are processed centrally at the Registrar (high-speed sorter + staff signature comparison from envelope images), per the EAP. The only in-person ballot scanning OC offers is an optional Ballot Drop Box Scanner pilot (started 2022 Primary) that lets a voter confirm a ballot was scanned before depositing it -- it does NOT verify signatures or do in-vote-center mail processing (https://ocvote.gov/vote-center-and-ballot-drop-box-accessibility/drop-box-scan).

California Voter's Choice Act (SB 450) vote-center model: all-mail ballots + Vote Centers replacing precinct polling places. Orange County Board of Supervisors voted to adopt on 2019-02-26 (board-vote date); first election conducted under VCA = March 3, 2020 Presidential Primary, with ~180+ Vote Centers countywide. Confirmed by CA SoS news release and OCROV EAP.

Pennsylvania does no signature MATCHING for mail/absentee ballots at all, so automated signature-verification software is necessarily not-adopted. In re November 3, 2020 General Election (Pa. Supreme Court, No. J-113-2020, decided Oct 23, 2020), a unanimous 7-0 decision, held that the PA Election Code contains no provision authorizing or requiring signature comparison and that 'county boards of elections are prohibited from rejecting absentee or mail-in ballots based on signature comparison conducted by county election officials or employees, or as the result of third-party challenges based on signature analysis and comparisons.' Petition brought by Sec. of the Commonwealth Kathy Boockvar. DOS guidance accordingly instructs counties to count ballots that bear a signature and matched valid voter ID at application, without comparing the envelope signature to the registration record. No Parascript/Verus/Bell & Howell-style automated signature-verification engine is used because the underlying manual practice is itself barred.

adoptions.first_election: 2016 (approx; earliest documented PA e-pollbook usage — 4 counties using e-pollbooks in 2016 per Votebeat/Spotlight PA. NOT the 2013 state-certification date.)
adoptions.vendor: KNOWiNK (Poll Pad, runs on Apple iPad COTS tablets)
adoptions.note: National vignette: KNOWiNK Poll Pad is the most widely used electronic pollbook in Pennsylvania (Votebeat/Spotlight PA, Oct 2025; $1,750/unit). VERIFIED against the cited DOS Voting Systems page, which lists successive KNOWiNK Poll Pad CERTIFICATIONS: v1.2 certified Oct 23, 2013; v1.3.3 May 30, 2018; v2.5.1 May 12, 2020; v3.1.0 Apr 1, 2022; v3.5.1 Feb 27, 2024. ADVERSARIAL CORRECTION: the Oct 23, 2013 date is a STATE CERTIFICATION/approval date, NOT a first-election deployment date — the DOS page provides only certification dates and does not say when any county first used the Poll Pad in an election. adopted_year was changed from 2013 (certification, disallowed as an adopted_year) to 2016, the earliest documented year of actual e-pollbook USAGE in PA elections per the cited Votebeat/Spotlight PA reporting ('just four counties were using e-pollbooks' in 2016). Confidence lowered primary->secondary because no cited source pins KNOWiNK's first-election year and the 2016 figure is attributed by the source to e-pollbooks generally (KNOWiNK is the most widely used device but not specifically named for the 2016 four). PA e-pollbook USAGE grew from 4 counties (2016) -> 10 (2020) -> 33 of 67 (Nov 2025), accelerated by Act 88 (2022), which distributes $45M/yr to counties for election administration (Votebeat confirms the 2016/2020/2025 figures, the $1,750/unit price, and the $45M/yr Act 88 figure). Three e-pollbook vendors certified/in use in PA per the DOS page: KNOWiNK, ES&S (ExpressPoll), Tenex (Precinct Central). Philadelphia tried KNOWiNK in 2019 but scrapped the contract weeks before Election Day over connectivity/reporting problems, then moved to ES&S in 2023 (Votebeat-confirmed). Lancaster County does not use e-pollbooks (Votebeat: >$400k to equip its 240 precincts); Mercer County purchased e-pollbooks for its 91 precincts in Aug 2025. e-pollbooks speed VOTER CHECK-IN, not ballot-tabulation speed.

'Sign, scan and go' is a California construct (AB 626 in-vote-center, envelope-free on-site mail-ballot signature-verify + scan) with no Pennsylvania analogue. PA has no vote-center model and no statutory mail-ballot signature verification at all (see asv finding), so there is no in-person mail-ballot signature-verify-and-scan workflow to adopt. PA mail ballots are returned by mail or to county drop-off/county election office and are pre-canvassed centrally beginning no earlier than 7 a.m. on Election Day under Act 77. Coverage of PA election operations (Votebeat/Spotlight PA 2025; WHYY explainer) describes precinct check-in via e-pollbooks and central mail-ballot canvassing, with no sign-scan-go process.

'Vote-center' here means the California Voter's Choice Act all-mail + any-vote-center model; Pennsylvania has no equivalent. PA votes at traditional assigned PRECINCT polling places (e.g., Mercer County's 91 precincts, Lancaster County's 240 precincts cited in the Oct 2025 Votebeat/Spotlight PA e-pollbook reporting). Act 77 (2019) created no-excuse mail voting and a permanent mail-ballot list but did NOT convert PA to a vote-center / all-mail model; in-person voters must vote at their assigned precinct. No Voter's Choice Act or vote-center adoption exists in PA.

No automated signature-verification (ASV) software/scanner found for Placer County. Placer's full certified stack in the CA SoS 'Voting Technologies in Use by County' lists (2024-2 and 2025-1) is Dominion ImageCast (ICC/ICE/ICX 5.10A) tabulation/BMD, Runbeck Sentio 9.2 ballot-on-demand, and Dominion ImageCast Remote 5.10A RAVBM -- there is no automated signature-matching product, and the SoS list has no ASV column. Returned vote-by-mail envelope signatures are compared by human review under CA Elections Code sec. 3019 (the Sign, Scan & Go program instead verifies the voter's signature at in-person check-in, which is not automated software matching of envelope signatures to the registration file). Finding by absence of evidence in the primary SoS technology list, hence secondary confidence.

adoptions.vendor: DFM (DFM Associates)
adoptions.note: CA SoS 'Voting Technologies in Use by County' lists the Electronic Pollbook for Placer as 'DFM' in both the Sept 19, 2024 (Nov 2024 General) and Oct 10, 2025 (Nov 2025 Special) editions -- i.e., DFM Associates' electronic roster used at vote centers for check-in. The Registrar's Sign, Scan & Go page describes electronic check-in: 'sign the roster at the check-in table.' Vote-center e-rosters came in with the VCA transition; first election = March 5, 2024. Pre-VCA, the SoS 'Voting Technology by County' list (As of May 9, 2022) showed Placer's e-pollbook column as 'Do not use,' confirming Placer had no electronic pollbook before adopting VCA. adopted_year is the first VCA election (2024), not a board/contract date.

adoptions.vendor: Dominion ImageCast Evolution (ICE) vote-center scanner; Placer 'Sign, Scan & Go' program under AB 626
adoptions.note: Placer County Registrar 'Sign, Scan & Go!' page: 'Sign, Scan & Go is Placer County's implementation of Assembly Bill 626, passed in 2023. Placer County first offered it in the 2024 primary and general elections, which together with the Voter's Choice Act, more than doubled the county's in-person voter turnout.' Process: 'Simply sign the roster at the check-in table, scan your ballot using the ballot scanner and GO!' -- the returned vote-by-mail ballot is verified at in-person check-in (signature on the roster, not deferred envelope-signature review) and scanned/counted in real time at the vote center so it is part of the Election Night tally. Enabling law AB 626 passed 2023 (law date); first election conducted with Sign, Scan & Go = March 5, 2024 Presidential Primary. Placer is the CA county most associated with AB 626 sign-scan-and-go. Confirmed secondarily by CapRadio (2024-11-01) and CBS Sacramento ('second year', 2025).

adoptions.vendor: Voter's Choice Act (SB 450) model; Dominion Voting Systems ImageCast tabulation (Dominion rebranded 'Liberty Vote'), Runbeck Sentio ballot-on-demand
adoptions.note: Placer County Registrar (Clerk-Recorder Elections) page: 'After years of research and planning, Placer County has made the decision to transition to the Voter's Choice Act (VCA) election model, starting with the 2024 Presidential Primary Election in March of 2024.' Board-vote/adoption date = June 13, 2023 ('The transition was approved by the Placer County Board of Supervisors on June 13, 2023.'; board item https://www.placer.ca.gov/DocumentCenter/View/70082/11A). first_election = March 5, 2024 Presidential Primary (first election conducted under the model). 17 vote centers in 2024 (per county press materials); EAP renewed in 2025. Approved 2025 Placer County Election Administration Plan is linked from the VCA page. SoS 'Voting Technologies in Use by County' (Sept 19, 2024) lists Placer tabulation as Dominion ICC 5.10A (central) / Dominion ICE 5.10A (vote-center scanner) / Dominion ICX 5.10A (accessible BMD), Runbeck Sentio 9.2 ballot-on-demand, Dominion ImageCast Remote 5.10A RAVBM.

adoptions.vendor: Automated signature-verification software embedded in the Registrar's high-speed mail sorter (BlueCrest Vantage sorter w/ Parascript SignatureXpert.AI engine per industry sources; not confirmed by a Riverside primary source)
adoptions.note: Automated signature verification (ASV) first used as a PILOT at the November 4, 2025 special election; moved to PRODUCTION at the June 2, 2026 Statewide Direct Primary. KESQ (2025-10-23, Registrar Art Tinoco demo): software in the Registrar's sorting machines compares the envelope signature to the voter's file in seconds, sorting ballots into ASV (passed) vs MSV (manual signature verification) buckets at a ~45% confidence threshold; during the pilot ALL ASV-passed ballots were still re-verified manually, with full implementation targeted for June 2, 2026 and an estimated >50% staffing reduction. Production use confirmed for June 2026: 'Riverside County introduced new software to help verify signatures... for the June 2026 primary election. If the system cannot verify a signature, it is reviewed by a staff member' (system passing ~50-60% as good signatures). Vendor/engine attribution (BlueCrest Vantage sorter + Parascript SignatureXpert.AI) is from industry/secondary sources, not a Riverside primary doc, so vendor confidence is secondary. ASV also operational in San Diego and San Bernardino counties (KESQ). Note: prior to ASV, the 2022 EAP describes purely manual signature comparison (staff compares scanned envelope image to signature on file).

adoptions.vendor: Registrar's election management system on networked laptops (no dedicated tablet e-pollbook product named)
adoptions.note: Electronic roster / real-time check-in adopted with the Voter's Choice Act transition; first conducted at the June 7, 2022 Gubernatorial Primary (first VCA election). EAP (Voting Technology > 'Direct Connection to Voter Database'): 'Instead of the former system which called for the creation of paper rosters of voters assigned to a particular polling place, this new voting method allows for a direct connection to the Registrar of Voters election management system. The Registrar of Voters utilizes five laptops located at the check-in station of each Vote Center... to search for the voter in the election management system... they are given an indication as to whether the voter has already voted or is eligible to cast a ballot.' This is a digital roster/e-pollbook in function (real-time voted/eligible status across all vote centers), but Riverside uses networked laptops tied to its EMS rather than a named tablet e-pollbook product (e.g. KNOWiNK Poll Pad / Tenex); vendor/product not named in the EAP. Before VCA (2022) Riverside used paper rosters at assigned polling places.

No in-vote-center mail-ballot 'Sign-Scan-and-Go' (AB 626 Placer-County-style) workflow. Returned vote-by-mail ballots are processed CENTRALLY at the Registrar: 'Each returned Vote-by-Mail ballot envelope is processed through a mail sorter which sorts the ballot by voting precinct. This process takes a picture of the envelope with the voter's signature, which is uploaded into the department's election management system. Staff then compares the scanned signature image to the signature on file...' (EAP). Ballots dropped at vote centers/drop boxes are transported to the Registrar for this central processing; there is no described in-vote-center verify-and-scan of the voter's own returned envelope. Sign-Scan-and-Go is a Placer County program, not Riverside. Finding by absence of evidence in the primary EAP.

adoptions.vendor: Dominion Voting Systems (Democracy Suite) ballot-marking + tabulation under the Voter's Choice Act vote-center model
adoptions.note: California Voter's Choice Act (SB 450) vote-center model: every voter mailed a ballot, expanded in-person early voting, any-vote-center voting, drop boxes. Riverside County Board of Supervisors approved VCA implementation on 2021-12-14 (board-vote / adoption date). First election conducted under the model = June 7, 2022 Gubernatorial Primary. EAP: 'The first official election under this new model will be the June 7, 2022 Gubernatorial Primary Election.' (Riverside ran vote centers in Nov 2020 and Sept 2021 under COVID emergency provisions, but formal VCA adoption/first VCA election = 2022.) Voting/tabulation system: Dominion Democracy Suite (ICX ballot-marking devices used exclusively, Ballot On-Demand, high-speed tabulators); system replaced in 2019 after state decertification.

No automated/software signature-matching found. EAP 'Life of a Ballot': returned VBM envelopes pass through 'an industrial mail sorter that date and timestamps the pink return envelope, while capturing the signature image,' then 'The signature image from the pink return envelope is compared to the voter's signature on file' and the voter is contacted if it does not match. The sorter captures images but no automated/AI matching engine (e.g., Parascript/Verus) is named anywhere in the EAP; comparison is described as a human review of scanned images = not automated signature verification. Tabulation = Dominion ICC 5.10A; ballot-on-demand printer = Runbeck Sentio 9.2 (per SoS Sept 2024 voting-tech table).

adoptions.vendor: DFM Associates EIMS (run on networked COTS check-in laptops)
adoptions.note: EAP: each Vote Center has 'Two check-in laptops to securely verify the status and eligibility of the voter,' connected over VPN to the EIMS voter database for real-time check-in across all centers (required by the vote-center model). Verified Voting documents Sacramento using DFM Associates EIMS as its e-pollbook (page shows a 'Sacramento County voter check in station'). NOTE: the CA SoS 'Voting Technologies in Use by County' table (Sept 19, 2024) lists Sacramento's 'Electronic Pollbook' column as 'Do not use' because the county runs the EIMS election-management system directly on laptops rather than a separately SoS-certified standalone e-pollbook product (e.g., Tenex/KNOWiNK). Adopted with the first VCA election, June 2018.

No AB 626 in-vote-center 'sign, scan and go' (envelope-free on-site mail-ballot signature-verify + scan) found. EAP lists Vote Center voter options as vote in person, pick up a replacement ballot, or 'Drop off a voted VBM ballot' (drop-off only; dropped VBM ballots are processed centrally with envelope/signature verification). AB 626 effective 2024; pioneered as 'sign, scan and go' by Placer County in 2024 — no equivalent Sacramento adoption documented.

Voter's Choice Act vote-center model. County EAP (2024-2027): 'The County of Sacramento was the largest of five pilot counties to adopt the VCA for the 2018' elections. First VCA election = June 5, 2018 statewide primary (other original pilots: San Mateo, Napa, Nevada, Madera). Corroborated by CA SoS VCA participating-counties list (https://www.sos.ca.gov/voters-choice-act).

Automatic Signature Verification (ASV) software is ADOPTED. County Registrar FAQ: mail ballot envelopes 'will be scanned on a mail sorting machine, capturing an image of your signature' which is compared by ASV software; the confidence threshold is set at 45% -- >=45% auto-counts, <45% routes to trained staff for manual review (https://elections.sbcounty.gov/faqs/what-is-asv/, https://elections.sbcounty.gov/faqs/mail-ballot-processing/). first_election = Nov 4, 2025 statewide special, by bracketing: a Sept 15, 2024 Black Voice News walkthrough of San Bernardino ballot processing described signature verification as fully MANUAL ('staff at computers examine and verify the signatures on the envelopes ... two points of verification') with NO ASV (archived: web.archive.org/web/20251228105949/https://blackvoicenews.com/2024/09/15/san-bernardino-county-registrar-voters/), while KESQ (Oct 23, 2025) reports ASV is 'already being used in San Diego and San Bernardino' as Riverside piloted it for the Nov 2025 special (https://kesq.com/news/2025/10/23/registrar-demonstrates-new-auto-signature-verification-tool-ahead-of-election/). The mail sorter hardware is a BlueCrest Vantage (county procedures list 'BlueCrest Vantage Mail Sorter'; sorter contract with DMT Solution Global dba BlueCrest approved by the Board on Dec 5, 2019), in use since ~2020 -- but the ASV signature-matching software is the new 2025 component, so adopted_year reflects the software, not the sorter. ASV software vendor not named by the county. confidence secondary because the exact first-election year is inferred (no explicit county 'first used Nov 2025' statement found), though the 'adopted' status itself is from the county's own FAQ (primary).

adoptions.vendor: KNOWiNK PollPad
adoptions.note: CA SoS 'Voting Technologies Used by Counties' (as of Oct 10, 2025) lists San Bernardino e-pollbook = KNOWiNK PollPad V 3.4.8 (tabulation Dominion ICC 5.19, BMD Dominion ICX 5.19, Runbeck Sentio 9.2 scanning, Democracy Live Secure Select RAVBM). First-use year pinned to the March 3, 2020 Presidential Primary because KNOWiNK PollPad already appears in the SoS 'Voting Technologies in use by County as of February 4, 2020' list for San Bernardino (https://votingsystems.cdn.sos.ca.gov/oversight/county-vsys/vot-tech-used-by-counties-2020-3.pdf), which corresponds to the county's new Dominion Democracy Suite rollout for the 2020 cycle. County polling page confirms in-use: 'you will be looked up on an Electronic Poll Pad' (https://elections.sbcounty.gov/voting/polls/). Earlier (pre-2020) e-pollbook use not affirmatively ruled out, but Feb 2020 SoS list is the first primary confirmation.

No in-vote-center mail-ballot 'Sign-Scan-and-Go' / AB 626 verify-and-scan workflow. San Bernardino is NOT a vote-center (VCA) county -- it runs traditional assigned/precinct polling places ('Vote at Your Assigned Polling Place on Election Day', https://elections.sbcounty.gov/voting/polls/), so there is no vote-center mail-processing model to host Sign-Scan-and-Go. Returned mail ballots are processed CENTRALLY at the Registrar via a BlueCrest Vantage high-speed sorter + ASV/manual signature verification, then extraction and tabulation (county Ballot Processing & Counting Procedures, https://elections.sbcounty.gov/community-outreach/observation/procedures/; Black Voice News Sep 2024 walkthrough). Marked secondary: absence-of-evidence of an in-vote-center verify+scan plus the structural fact that the county has no vote centers.

San Bernardino has NOT adopted the California Voter's Choice Act (SB 450) vote-center model. The county Registrar instructs voters to 'Vote at Your Assigned Polling Place on Election Day' and look up an assigned precinct location (https://elections.sbcounty.gov/voting/polls/) -- the precinct model, not 'vote at any vote center.' San Bernardino does not appear on the CA SoS list of VCA participating counties (https://www.sos.ca.gov/voters-choice-act). The county does offer early/in-person voting locations, but ballots are precinct-assigned, not the all-mail + any-vote-center VCA model.

Automated signature verification: high-speed sorter cameras photograph the envelope signature and compute a confidence match; ballots are sorted into 'ASV' (auto-passed) and 'MSV' (manual) buckets, with anything below ~45% confidence routed to human review (fox5sandiego/NBC7 2024 coverage, KPBS Oct 30 2024). adopted_year is approximate: the Sept 5, 2023 EAP still described staff performing the signature comparison from captured images (no auto-matching), while 2024 election-cycle reporting describes the automated confidence-scoring system in use; KESQ (Oct 23 2025) confirms San Diego 'already' uses ASV. Could not pin first election to March 2024 primary vs Nov 2024 general from primary sources; ASV software vendor not identified (mail sorters are Runbeck-class). NOT in CA SoS certified-voting-system list because ASV sorter software is not a 'voting system.'

adoptions.vendor: Tenex Precinct Central V 6.0.1
adoptions.note: CA SoS 'Voting Technologies Used by Counties' (Nov 5 2024) lists San Diego e-pollbook = Tenex Precinct Central V 6.0.1. E-pollbooks replaced paper rosters with the Voter's Choice Act vote-center transition; first used at the June 7, 2022 Gubernatorial Primary (board approved the vote-center model Oct 19, 2021). Confirmed in San Diego EAP (09/05/2023): 'Electronic pollbooks (e-pollbooks) replaced paper rosters.' No evidence of e-pollbook use before the 2022 VCA transition.

No evidence San Diego implemented an in-vote-center mail-ballot signature-verify-and-scan ('Sign-Scan-and-Go' / AB 626) model. The EAP (09/05/2023) describes returned mail ballots being processed centrally at the Registrar's office via high-speed sorters and signature verification, not verified-and-scanned at the vote center on drop-off. AB 626 (chaptered Oct 2023) only permits the practice; no San Diego adoption found. Marked estimated because this is absence-of-evidence rather than an explicit county statement of non-adoption.

San Diego County Board of Supervisors approved the move to the Voter's Choice Act vote-center model on Oct 19, 2021; the first election conducted under it was the June 7, 2022 Gubernatorial Primary. Also listed on the CA SoS Voter's Choice Act counties page (2022 cohort). Every active registered voter mailed a ballot; in-person voting at any vote center using e-pollbooks.

No automated signature-verification software. SF DOE processing 'begins with capturing an image of the signature on each ballot envelope for comparison with the signature in a voter's registration record'; staff then compare by eye ('If the signatures compare, the Department can open the envelope and remove the ballot cards for scanning'). The imaging equipment only captures the signature image -- it does not auto-match. Before any signature-based challenge, the envelope signature is reviewed by three different staff members and compared to all signatures in the voter's record (SF voter guide / DOE FAQ, sf.gov vote-by-mail). Humans comparing scanned images by eye = ASV not-adopted (a finding, with evidence).

CONTROL: San Francisco does NOT deploy electronic pollbooks at its polling places. It runs the traditional neighborhood-precinct model -- 501 polling places (Nov 5, 2024) -- where each voter is checked in against a PAPER roster at their assigned precinct (voters not on the paper roster vote provisionally). The CA SoS 'Voting Technologies in Use by County' list (Oct 10, 2025) shows San Francisco's Electronic Pollbook column = 'DFM' (DFM Associates EIMS / Vote Center App). EIMS is an election-management system, not a tablet e-pollbook; per Verified Voting it 'can be used in the same manner as an electronic poll book' and SF uses it for back-office / City Hall Voting Center in-person check-in against VoteCal -- it is NOT a precinct e-pollbook deployment. The project's own county_speed dataset records San Francisco epollbook=false. (Verified Voting on DFM EIMS, secondary: https://verifiedvoting.org/election-system/dfm-associates-eims/.) SF voting/tabulation system is Dominion (ICC 5.19 central, ICE 5.19A precinct, ICX 5.19 BMD), Democracy Live Secure Select for remote-accessible VBM -- recorded as context, not one of the four tracked types. [VERIFIED 2026-06: opened the cited CA SoS PDF (As of Oct 10, 2025). SF's row reads Central Tabulation = Dominion ICC 5.19, Polling Place Tabulator = Dominion ICE 5.19A, Accessibility = Dominion ICX 5.19, Remote Accessible VBM = Democracy Live Secure Select, and Electronic Pollbook = 'DFM' -- NOT 'Do not use' (the value other counties with no e-pollbook show). Because the cited primary SoS doc literally lists a 'DFM' e-pollbook product for SF, it does not by itself establish 'not-adopted'; that determination rests on the interpretation (Verified Voting, SECONDARY) that DFM Associates EIMS is a back-office / City Hall Voting Center system rather than a precinct tablet e-pollbook. Corroborated operationally by sf.gov/vote-in-person, which shows in-person check-in against a PAPER roster at all 501 assigned polling places. Status kept not-adopted (operationally true -- paper rosters, no precinct e-pollbook); confidence downgraded primary -> secondary because the cited primary source does not cleanly support the not-adopted claim.]

No in-vote-center mail-ballot sign-verify-and-scan (AB 626 'Sign, Scan & Go'). San Francisco is not a Voter's Choice Act / vote-center county -- it has no vote centers, so there is no on-the-spot mail-ballot verify+scan. Returned VBM ballots are processed centrally at the Department of Elections (signature imaged, human-verified, extracted, tabulated on central Dominion ICC scanners). Election-Day in-person voting uses precinct tabulators (Dominion ICE) at 501 traditional polling places, plus the City Hall Voting Center for early voting. No documentation of a Sign-Scan-Go workflow.

CONTROL: San Francisco has NOT adopted the California Voter's Choice Act (all-mailed-ballot + vote-center model). It retains the traditional neighborhood polling-place model -- 501 precinct polling places assigned to voters on Election Day (Nov 5, 2024), open 7am-8pm, plus a single City Hall Voting Center for 29-day early in-person voting. Voters must go to their assigned polling place (or vote a provisional ballot elsewhere). SF is absent from the CA SoS VCA county adoption list; it is one of the few Bay Area counties (with Contra Costa and Solano) that never adopted VCA.

FIRMED UP (2026-07-09, Task 12): the county's own official 'Signature "Cure" Letters' page (evidence_url, verified by curl fetch) states: 'The following information is provided pursuant to EC section 3019 regarding how to "cure" noncomparable and missing signatures on ballot identification envelopes,' i.e. SLO runs the standard statutory human-cure workflow (Elections Code 3019), not a description of any automated signature-matching product. Independently, KSBY's 'San Luis Obispo County introduces new tech for upcoming elections' (the same article cited for the epollbook row above; verified by curl fetch) describes SLO's one piece of signature-adjacent hardware in enough operational detail to rule out ASV directly: the Agilis Election Mail Sorting and Processing System (Runbeck) 'quickly sends mail-in ballots past a camera, which scans the ballots for voter signatures, then separates them into groups of 200. If any mail-in ballot envelopes have an abnormality, like a missing signature, that envelope will be separated from the group so poll workers can double-check it. Once those signatures are verified, the ballots are sent through the machine again to be opened.' This is an imaging/sorting machine that FLAGS anomalies for a human poll worker to verify by eye; it does not itself perform algorithmic signature matching (contrast with true ASV products like Parascript, which auto-score the match and only escalate low-confidence cases to a human). No mention of any signature-matching software in county materials, procurement records, or news coverage found in this pass. Confidence held at secondary (both citations are, respectively, an official process page that does not explicitly rule out software and a news description of hardware, not a registrar statement saying 'we do not use ASV'), but this is a firmer, more specific pair of primary-adjacent citations than the previous pass's generic 'absence of evidence' finding (which cited only the vote-by-mail overview page, a page that does not in fact discuss the verification process at all upon re-check).

San Luis Obispo adopted electronic pollbooks for the FIRST TIME at the June 2, 2026 Statewide Direct Primary -- the county had been a paper-roster holdout. County Clerk-Recorder Elaina Cano: 'we're one of the few counties left that don't use [e-pollbooks]' (KSBY, 'SLO County introduces new tech for upcoming elections'). Deputy Director/Registrar Penny Boyd (KSBY, 'New E-poll books coming to San Luis Obispo County polling locations'): e-pollbooks replace the paper rosters voters signed at each polling location, give poll workers real-time access to voter registration to confirm a voter is registered and at the correct assigned polling place, save ~$100,000/yr in roster printing, and cut provisional ballots. adopted_year=2026 = first election conducted with the tech (June 2, 2026). VENDOR not yet publicly disclosed in coverage; the only SoS-certified e-pollbooks in CA are KNOWiNK PollPad and Tenex Precinct Central, so SLO's product is almost certainly one of those, but neither news nor a primary procurement doc naming it was located. The CA SoS 'Voting Technologies in Use by County' (as of Oct 10, 2025) still lists SLO's e-pollbook column as 'Do not use', consistent with the e-pollbook being brand-new for 2026 and post-dating that list. VERIFIED: the evidence_url KSBY article confirms e-pollbooks are used 'for the first time' at the June 2 Primary and quotes Clerk-Recorder Cano ('We're one of the few counties left that don't use [e-pollbooks]'); adopted_year=2026 = first-election year is correct. The article does NOT quote Penny Boyd and names NO vendor (the Boyd detail above comes from a separate KSBY piece, not this evidence_url). Confidence downgraded primary->secondary because adoption is supported ONLY by KSBY news; no official registrar/SoS adoption doc exists yet (the Oct 2025 SoS voting-tech list still shows SLO e-pollbook as 'Do not use').

No in-vote-center mail-ballot signature-verify-and-scan ('Sign, Scan & Go', AB 626) capability. SLO is NOT a Voter's Choice Act county and runs traditional precinct polling places, not vote centers (KSBY e-pollbook coverage repeatedly references voters' 'assigned polling location'). Returned/dropped vote-by-mail ballots are transported to the central Elections Office for scanning, signature verification, and tabulation -- there is no on-site verify-and-scan-at-the-vote-center workflow. AB 626 Sign-Scan-Go is associated with Placer County, not SLO. Finding by absence of evidence in a non-VCA, central-processing county.

San Luis Obispo has NOT adopted the Voter's Choice Act all-mail/vote-center model. SLO does not appear on the CA Secretary of State 'VCA Participating Counties' list, and the county continues to operate traditional precinct-based polling places (where the new 2026 e-pollbooks are being deployed), with vote-by-mail processed centrally at the Elections Office. SLO has been eligible to adopt VCA since Jan 1, 2018 but has not done so.

adoptions.vendor: Pitney Bowes/BlueCrest Vantage Ballot Sorter (imaging/sorting only)
adoptions.note: No automated signature-verification software. The county's 'Counting Vote by Mail Ballots' page states a Vantage Ballot Sorter takes a picture of the signature, then 'A team compares the signature on the envelope with the one on file.' Signature matching is done by human staff comparing scanned images by eye -- the sorter only images/sorts, it does not auto-approve matches. This is a not-adopted finding for automated signature verification.

adoptions.vendor: Tenex Precinct Central V 6.0.1
adoptions.note: CA SoS 'Voting Technologies in Use by County' (as of Oct 10, 2025) lists San Mateo's electronic pollbook as 'Tenex Precinct Central V 6.0.1'. E-pollbooks were first deployed for the June 5, 2018 VCA vote centers (each vote center has 3-7 e-pollbooks for check-in, per smcacre.gov vote-centers page). The Tenex version is the current certified listing; the specific e-pollbook vendor used in the very first (June 2018) deployment was not independently confirmed.

No evidence of in-vote-center mail-ballot signature-verify-and-scan (AB 626 'Sign, Scan & Go'). Returned mail ballots are processed centrally: imaged/sorted on the Vantage Ballot Sorter, signature-compared by a team, extracted at extraction desks, and tabulated on central Dominion scanners (7 scanning stations incl. two HiPro). Vote-center mail-ballot drop-offs are transported to the central facility, not verified+scanned on the spot.

San Mateo was one of the original counties to adopt the California Voter's Choice Act (all-mailed-ballot + vote-center model). The Board of Supervisors authorized the Chief Elections Officer on Sept 12, 2017 to conduct the June 5, 2018 Statewide Direct Primary as the county's first election under the VCA -- the board-authorization date is Sept 12, 2017; first election conducted with the model was June 5, 2018.

Cited county 'How Your Vote Is Counted' page states Vote-by-Mail ballots 'have to be signature-verified' but describes NO automated signature-matching/recognition software, scanner, or envelope-imaging sorter. (Adversarial re-check 2026-06: the earlier claim that the Registrar runs a high-speed sorter that images envelopes for on-screen comparison is NOT supported by the cited source and was removed.) With no cited evidence that the county runs ASV software, ASV is classified not-adopted per the project default (unconfirmed signature-recognition software = manual/by-eye review). Confidence secondary because the determination is inferred from the absence of any ASV mention; the cited source does not actually specify the verification method (human-by-eye vs software).

adoptions.vendor: KNOWiNK PollPad (2020-2024); switched to Tenex Precinct Central V6.1.2 by 2025
adoptions.note: Santa Clara deployed e-pollbooks when it converted to the Voter's Choice Act vote-center model for the March 3, 2020 Presidential Primary (first VCA election). CA SoS 'Voting Technologies in Use by County' lists Santa Clara's e-pollbook as KNOWiNK PollPad (V2.5.2.01b in 2021, V3.0.1 in 2022 & 2024) and then Tenex Precinct Central V6.1.2 in the 2025 certified list, i.e. the county switched e-pollbook vendor from KNOWiNK to Tenex by 2025. County 'How the County of Santa Clara Protects our Votes' page confirms E-pollbooks reconcile signatures vs ballots cast at Vote Centers.

No in-vote-center 'Sign-Scan-and-Go' mail-ballot processing. Vote-by-mail ballots returned to Vote Centers / drop boxes are transported to the central Registrar of Voters office where signature verification and tabulation occur during the canvass; the county does not describe verifying signatures and scanning mail ballots in real time at the vote center. No AB 626-style sign-scan-go program found for Santa Clara.

County converted to the Voter's Choice Act vote-center model in 2020: 'Beginning with the 2020 elections, County of Santa Clara voters have a more modern voting experience under the Voter's Choice Act. In-person voting takes place at larger Vote Centers.' First VCA election was the March 3, 2020 Presidential Primary (~110 Vote Centers, 10-day and 3-day early voting). Confirmed by the county Election Administration Plan filed with CA SoS (santa-clara-amended-eap-11.22.19.pdf).

No automated signature-verification software found. Ventura's EAP describes human review only: 'Once a VBM ballot is returned to the Elections Division, the VBM ballot envelope is signature checked against the voter's signature(s) on their voter file and any supporting documents. The VBM ballot is not processed until Elections Division staff determines the signature is a match.' The companion VCA FAQ (clerkrecorder.venturacounty.gov/wp-content/uploads/2022/03/VCA_FAQ.pdf) uses identical staff-comparison language. No ASV vendor or scanner-matching software named anywhere in county materials found in this pass.

adoptions.vendor: County of Ventura Elections Division proprietary EIMS (Election Information Management System), VoteCal-integrated, on a hardened laptop at each Vote Center
adoptions.note: Ventura's Election Administration Plan Renewal (July 2023, elections.cdn.sos.ca.gov/vca/eap/ventura-eap.pdf, p.5 'VOTING TECHNOLOGY') states: 'VCA voter look-up technology utilizes a combination of electronic hardware and software to ensure secure electronic communication between the Vote Centers and the County of Ventura Elections Division's Election Information Management System (EIMS). EIMS interfaces directly with the Secretary of State's office through VoteCal... This VCA technology operates on a laptop that is hardened.' The EAP also states a returned ballot is 'automatically voided' at every other Vote Center once cast at any one -- a real-time cross-location voter-status check, the defining function of an e-pollbook. This adopted with Ventura's first VCA election, the June 7, 2022 Statewide Direct Primary (county press release: 'Ventura County's first full Voter's Choice Act election'; LWVC: Secretary of State Weber's 2022 new-VCA-county announcement names Ventura among 11 counties adopting VCA in 2022). DOCUMENTED CONFLICT, do not silently resolve: the CA SoS's own 'Voting Technologies in Use by County' survey (as of Oct 10, 2025, https://votingsystems.cdn.sos.ca.gov/oversight/county-vsys/vot-tech-by-counties-2025-1.pdf) lists Ventura's 'Electronic Pollbook' column as 'Do not use', while the SAME row shows a real 'Ballot on Demand VoteCenter/Polling Place' device ('Dominion MBP 5.19'), i.e. Ventura is clearly running the VCA vote-center model with on-demand ballot printing but is not credited with one of the SoS's tracked NAMED commercial e-pollbook products (e.g. KNOWiNK, DFM EIMS/Vote Center App). Confidence held at 'secondary' (not 'primary') specifically because two primary-grade sources disagree on whether Ventura's proprietary look-up system counts as an 'electronic pollbook' in the state's own taxonomy; a verifier should re-check with the Ventura Elections Division directly which classification is current. (Cf. the project's own SF/DFM caveat: a snapshot survey taken well after the elections in question can miss or misclassify a county's actual practice.)

No in-Vote-Center mail-ballot verify-and-scan (AB 626 'Sign-Scan-and-Go') program found. Ventura's EAP states plainly: 'All voted ballots are returned nightly to the Elections Division. Tabulation is performed at the Elections Division and there are no tabulators at any Vote Centers.' This directly rules out any Vote Center-side scan/tabulate step (the model piloted elsewhere, e.g. Placer). Confidence primary: the county's own EAP affirmatively describes the opposite (central-only tabulation), not merely an absence of evidence.

Ventura adopted the Voter's Choice Act (VCA) vote-center model in 2022 (SoS Weber's 2022 announcement names Ventura among 11 new VCA counties that year, alongside Alameda, Marin, Merced, Riverside, San Benito, San Diego, Santa Cruz, Sonoma, Stanislaus, and Yolo). Ventura's own registrar press release titles the June 7, 2022 Statewide Direct Primary Election 'Ventura County's first full Voter's Choice Act election' (51 Vote Centers open by Election Day, staggered 10-day/3-day openings starting May 28). Ventura was NOT on the CA SoS's 2020 VCA-county report (the 15-county list: Amador, Butte, Calaveras, El Dorado, Fresno, Los Angeles, Madera, Mariposa, Napa, Nevada, Orange, Sacramento, San Mateo, Santa Clara, Tuolumne), confirming 2022 as the correct first adoption year, not an earlier pilot. Ventura's first EAP was adopted in 2022 and renewed July 2023 (Final Revision 7-6-23, elections.cdn.sos.ca.gov/vca/eap/ventura-eap.pdf).

Wisconsin does not use automated signature-verification software, and indeed does not do signature MATCHING at all. Absentee security in WI is photo-ID-on-request plus a witnessed certificate envelope: the voter signs and an adult U.S.-citizen witness signs and provides an address on the absentee certificate. Clerks check that the required voter and witness signatures/address are PRESENT (and that ID is on file) — they do not compare the envelope signature against a reference signature on record, by eye or by machine. 'Wisconsin doesn't verify your signature in the way some other states do.' No ASV vendor or signature-matching step exists statewide. The Badger Book e-pollbook captures an in-person voter's signature on screen as the poll-list signature but performs no signature comparison/verification. Confidence secondary (structural/legal absence of a signature-matching regime; no ASV product anywhere in WI).

adoptions.vendor: Wisconsin Elections Commission (in-house; 'Badger Book' application, runs on a Microsoft Surface-style tablet + printer station)
adoptions.note: National vignette, not a CA county. 'Badger Book' is the only electronic poll book authorized for use in Wisconsin and is designed/maintained in-house by the Wisconsin Elections Commission (WEC) — the only e-poll book with indirect WisVote (statewide voter file) integration. WEC page: 'In 2017, at the direction of the Commission, WEC staff developed an electronic poll book application for use in Wisconsin elections.' It is an electronic version of the paper poll book: used to check in voters, process Election Day Registrations (Wis. Stat. 6.79(2)(c)), and record absentee participation; it does NOT tabulate votes and is not connected to the internet. adopted_year=2018 = first ELECTION conducted with it: five Badger Books were piloted at five polling places (cities of Beloit, Brookfield, Mequon, Sun Prairie and the town of Trenton) in the April 3, 2018 Spring Election, checking in/registering 5,000+ voters (Wisconsin State Journal / madison.com, https://madison.com/wsj/news/local/ask/catching-up/catching-up-electronic-poll-book-pilot-a-success-sun-prairie/article_ec153502-9180-59ce-bb75-dad2fc9be0ab.html). Sun Prairie was the first municipality to adopt it (2018). The 2017 date is the board-direction/development year (in note, not adopted_year). Adoption has since grown to ~400 of Wisconsin's ~1,850 municipalities (Votebeat, June 2025).

'Sign-Scan-and-Go' is a California program (AB 626): in-vote-center, on-the-spot mail-ballot signature verification + scan/tabulation. Wisconsin has no analog. WI has no Voter's Choice Act vote centers, and absentee/mail ballots are not signature-verified-and-scanned on demand at a polling place — they are returned to the municipal clerk and processed/counted by election inspectors at the polling place or at a designated central count facility on Election Day. The Badger Book records absentee participation but does not scan or tabulate ballots ('At no point do Badger Books interact with the voting equipment... Badger Books do not process votes'). Not-adopted; confidence secondary (the program is CA-specific with no Wisconsin equivalent).

'Vote-center' here means the California Voter's Choice Act (SB 450) all-mail / any-eligible-voter-can-vote-at-any-center model. Wisconsin has no such statute or model: voters are assigned to a specific neighborhood polling place by ward/municipality and must check in there (the Badger Book verifies a voter against the WisVote roster at their assigned polling place). In-person absentee ('early') voting occurs at the municipal clerk's office or designated alternate sites, not at interchangeable VCA-style vote centers. Not-adopted; confidence secondary (the VCA vote-center model is CA-specific and absent in WI).
